---
title: "Air Quality by City"
description: "Pick a city and see how its air pollutants have changed since 2005 — annual trends and the month-by-month detail, straight from the NAPS monitoring network"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import os
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date

# Pollutant panels (label, units) and their grid positions.
POLLS = [("PM2.5", "µg/m³"), ("O3", "ppb"), ("NO2", "ppb"), ("SO2", "ppb"), ("CO", "ppm")]
POS = [(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 1), (2, 2)]
PREFERRED = ["Toronto", "Montréal", "Vancouver", "Calgary", "Edmonton",
             "Ottawa–Gatineau", "Winnipeg", "Québec City"]
ANN = "data/environment/naps_city_annual.csv"
MON = "data/environment/naps_city_monthly.csv"


def _ordered_cities(df):
    present = set(df["city"])
    head = [c for c in PREFERRED if c in present]
    return head + sorted(present - set(head))


def city_dashboard(df, xcol, *, mode, hovx, source_note, who_line=False):
    """Small-multiples (5 pollutant panels) with a city dropdown that toggles
    which city's series are shown across all panels."""
    cities = _ordered_cities(df)
    fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=3,
                        subplot_titles=[f"{p} ({u})" for p, u in POLLS],
                        horizontal_spacing=0.07, vertical_spacing=0.15)
    trace_city = []
    for city in cities:
        for (p, u), (rr, cc) in zip(POLLS, POS):
            s = df[(df["city"] == city) & (df["pollutant"] == p)].sort_values(xcol)
            fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
                x=s[xcol], y=s["value"], mode=mode,
                line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=2),
                marker=dict(size=4) if "markers" in mode else dict(),
                showlegend=False, visible=(city == cities[0]),
                hovertemplate=f"{p} {hovx}: %{{y:.1f}} {u}<extra></extra>"),
                row=rr, col=cc)
            trace_city.append(city)
    if who_line:   # WHO 2021 annual PM2.5 guideline on the PM2.5 panel
        fig.add_hline(y=5, line_dash="dot", line_color="#2ca02c", opacity=0.7, row=1, col=1)
    buttons = [dict(method="update", label=c,
                    args=[{"visible": [tc == c for tc in trace_city]}])
               for c in cities]
    fig.update_xaxes(gridcolor="#e0e0e0")
    fig.update_yaxes(gridcolor="#e0e0e0", rangemode="tozero")
    fig.update_layout(
        height=620, plot_bgcolor="white", margin=dict(t=90, b=80, l=45, r=20),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, active=0, x=0.5, xanchor="center",
                          y=1.20, yanchor="top", bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc",
                          borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        annotations=list(fig.layout.annotations) + [
            dict(text="Select a city", xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0.5, y=1.27,
                 xanchor="center", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555")),
            dict(text=source_note, xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.13,
                 showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    return fig
```

Pick a city and see how the air its residents breathe has changed since 2005. These
are the actual measurements from Environment and Climate Change Canada's
[National Air Pollution Surveillance (NAPS)](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/air-pollution/monitoring-networks-data/national-air-pollution-program.html)
network — the monitoring stations behind the national indicators on the
[Air Quality](air-quality.qmd) page — averaged across each city's stations. The
broad pattern is improvement: sulphur dioxide and nitrogen dioxide have fallen
sharply in most cities. The exception is fine particulate matter (PM2.5), which has
been pushed up in recent years by wildfire smoke — most visibly in the western
cities in 2023.

## Annual trends

Annual mean concentration for each pollutant, 2005 onward. Use the dropdown to
change city. The dotted green line on the PM2.5 panel is the World Health
Organization's 2021 guideline (5 µg/m³).

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(ANN):
    a = pd.read_csv(ANN)
    fig = city_dashboard(
        a, "year", mode="lines+markers", hovx="%{x}", who_line=True,
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, NAPS Annual Summaries (1-hour basis), stations averaged per city | Data as of: {get_data_date(ANN)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("NAPS city data not yet available. Run: python -m pipeline.build_naps_cities")
```

## Month-by-month detail

The same pollutants at monthly resolution, which reveals the seasonal rhythm —
summer ozone, winter particulates — and the sharp spikes when wildfire smoke
arrives. Switch to a western city like Edmonton or Calgary and look at the PM2.5
panel around 2023 to see a record smoke season in the monthly data.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(MON):
    m = pd.read_csv(MON)
    m["date"] = pd.to_datetime(m["date"])
    fig = city_dashboard(
        m, "date", mode="lines", hovx="%{x|%b %Y}",
        source_note=f"Source: Environment and Climate Change Canada, NAPS Annual Summaries (monthly means, 1-hour basis), stations averaged per city | Data as of: {get_data_date(MON)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("NAPS monthly data not yet available. Run: python -m pipeline.build_naps_cities")
```

## Download Data

- [Air quality by city — annual (CSV)](../data/environment/naps_city_annual.csv) — ECCC NAPS Annual Summaries
- [Air quality by city — monthly (CSV)](../data/environment/naps_city_monthly.csv) — ECCC NAPS Annual Summaries

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## About these data

**Source & method.** Values come from the NAPS network's published Annual
Summaries — per-station validated annual and monthly means on a 1-hour basis. For
each city we average all of its stations, so a city with several monitors is
smoothed across them. Units are µg/m³ for PM2.5 and ppb for the gases (ppm for CO).

**Coverage.** About 30 of Canada's larger cities are included, 2005–2024. Smaller
communities with NAPS monitors are omitted here to keep the selector manageable;
the full network is larger. Validated NAPS data lags the calendar year by roughly
12–18 months, so the most recent year may update.

**Cities vs. the national picture.** These city averages complement the
population-weighted *national* indicator on the [Air Quality](air-quality.qmd)
page (which is the standard reference but only national and from 2009). Here the
trade-off is reversed: city-level and back to 2005, but a simple station average
rather than a population-weighted exposure estimate. All data are published under
the Open Government Licence – Canada.
:::
